concepts.name: System of Linear Equations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Several straight-line conditions that must all hold at once — the solution is where all the lines (or planes) cross.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite set of equations, each linear in the unknowns; the solution set — where all equations hold simultaneously — is empty, a single point, or an infinite family. The later matrix view writes the system as Ax = b, making the solution set an affine subspace.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Systems of equations
MIT OCW 18.06 Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 1: The Geometry of Linear Equations

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 2

concepts.name: Gaussian Elimination
concepts.intuition_gloss: Systematically cancel variables row by row until the system becomes a staircase you can read the answer off of.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm reducing a matrix to (reduced) row echelon form via elementary row operations, solving Ax = b and revealing rank and pivot structure.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 2: Elimination with Matrices
Wikipedia — Gaussian elimination
Matrix Calculator (step-by-step row reduction)

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 2
Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra lecture 20

concepts.name: Span & Linear Combination
concepts.intuition_gloss: Everywhere you can reach by stretching and adding a given set of arrows — their combined territory.
concepts.formal_gloss: The span of a set S in a vector space is the set of all finite linear combinations of elements of S; it is the smallest subspace containing S.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2

concepts.name: Linear Independence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A set of arrows where none is redundant — you can't build any one of them out of the others.
concepts.formal_gloss: Vectors v1..vn are linearly independent if c1v1 + ... + cnvn = 0 implies every ci = 0.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors
Khan Academy — Linear independence

concepts.name: Basis
concepts.intuition_gloss: A minimal set of reference arrows from which every vector in the space can be built exactly one way.
concepts.formal_gloss: A linearly independent set that spans the vector space; every vector has a unique expansion in a basis.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many independent directions a space has — the number of knobs you need to specify a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The cardinality of any basis of a vector space, well-defined because all bases of a space have equal cardinality.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2
3Blue1Brown — Abstract vector spaces

concepts.name: Subspace
concepts.intuition_gloss: A flat sheet or line through the origin living inside a bigger space, closed off as a little vector world of its own.
concepts.formal_gloss: A subset of a vector space containing 0 and closed under addition and scalar multiplication, hence a vector space itself.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06 video lectures (see Lectures 5-6: vector spaces and subspaces)
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 1

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiply matching coordinates and add — a single number measuring how much two arrows point the same way.
concepts.formal_gloss: For u, v in R^n, u·v = Σ u_i v_i = |u||v|cos θ; the standard inner product on R^n.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Dot products and duality
Khan Academy — Vectors and spaces

concepts.name: Cross Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: From two arrows in 3D, build a third one at right angles to both, whose length is the area of the parallelogram the two trace out.
concepts.formal_gloss: The bilinear operation on R³ with u × v orthogonal to both u and v, |u × v| = |u||v|sin θ, and direction given by the right-hand rule; componentwise it is the formal determinant with rows (i, j, k), u, v.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Cross products
Wikipedia — Cross product

sources: standard calculus III and intro linear algebra curricula

concepts.name: Norm & Distance
concepts.intuition_gloss: The length of an arrow — and with it, a ruler for measuring how far apart two vectors are.
concepts.formal_gloss: The Euclidean length ||v|| = √(v·v) of a vector in R^n, satisfying positivity, homogeneity ||cv|| = |c|·||v||, and the triangle inequality; these three properties, taken as axioms on a general vector space, define the abstract norms studied via inner-product and Banach spaces.

resources.title: Wikipedia — Norm (mathematics)
3Blue1Brown — Vectors

concepts.name: Orthogonality
concepts.intuition_gloss: Directions at perfect right angles — moving along one contributes nothing at all to the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Vectors u and v are orthogonal when their inner product is zero; orthogonal complements decompose a space into perpendicular parts.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 14: Orthogonal vectors and subspaces
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 6

concepts.name: Inner Product Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any vector world — including spaces of functions or signals — given its own version of angle and length.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector space with a positive-definite form ⟨u,v⟩ that is symmetric and bilinear in the real case, and conjugate-symmetric and sesquilinear in the complex case, generalizing the dot product; completing it yields a Hilbert space.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 6
Wikipedia — Inner product space

concepts.name: Orthogonal Projection
concepts.intuition_gloss: Drop a shadow of a vector straight down onto a subspace — the closest point to it that the subspace contains.
concepts.formal_gloss: The linear map P onto a subspace W along its orthogonal complement, satisfying P² = P = P*; Pv is the unique nearest point of W to v.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 15: Projections onto subspaces
Khan Academy — Orthogonal projections (Alternate coordinate systems)

concepts.name: Gram–Schmidt Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: Straighten a skewed set of arrows one at a time — subtract each one's shadow on the previous ones, then rescale to length one.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm converting a linearly independent list into an orthonormal list with the same span, by successive orthogonal projection and normalization; it is the algorithm underlying the QR factorization (see matrix-factorization).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 17: Orthogonal matrices and Gram-Schmidt
Wikipedia — Gram–Schmidt process

concepts.name: Determinant
concepts.intuition_gloss: The volume-scaling factor of a transformation — how much it inflates or squashes space, with a sign for flipping orientation.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique alternating multilinear function of a square matrix's columns with det(I) = 1; nonzero iff the matrix is invertible, and det(AB) = det(A)det(B).

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — The determinant
MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 18: Properties of determinants

concepts.name: Matrix Inverse
concepts.intuition_gloss: The transformation that exactly undoes another — run the stretch-and-rotate movie backwards.
concepts.formal_gloss: For square A, the matrix A⁻¹ with AA⁻¹ = A⁻¹A = I; it exists iff det(A) ≠ 0, iff A has full rank.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Inverse matrices, column space and null space
Matrix Calculator

sources: invertible matrix theorem in standard texts (Lay, Strang)

concepts.name: Trace
concepts.intuition_gloss: Add up a matrix's diagonal and you get a number that doesn't care which coordinates you drew — the combined total of its stretch factors.
concepts.formal_gloss: The sum of the diagonal entries of a square matrix; basis-invariant (tr(P⁻¹AP) = tr A), satisfies tr(AB) = tr(BA), and equals the sum of the eigenvalues counted with algebraic multiplicity.

resources.title: Wikipedia — Trace (linear algebra)
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8

concepts.name: Rank
concepts.intuition_gloss: The number of genuinely independent directions a matrix's output can fill — how much of the target space it actually reaches.
concepts.formal_gloss: The dimension of the column space (equivalently row space) of a matrix, equal to the number of pivots in its echelon form.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06 video lectures (see Lectures 7-8: solving Ax = 0 and Ax = b, rank)
Wikipedia — Rank (linear algebra)

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 3

concepts.name: Kernel & Image
concepts.intuition_gloss: What a transformation crushes to zero, and everything it can actually produce — the two subspaces that tell its whole story.
concepts.formal_gloss: For linear T: V → W, ker T = {v : Tv = 0} is a subspace of V and im T = {Tv : v ∈ V} is a subspace of W.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Inverse matrices, column space and null space
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3

concepts.name: Rank–Nullity Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A conservation law for dimensions: the directions a map crushes plus the directions that survive in its output always add back up to the space you started with.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a linear map T on a finite-dimensional space V, dim ker T + dim im T = dim V.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3
Wikipedia — Rank–nullity theorem

sources: Axler LADR ch. 3 ('fundamental theorem of linear maps')

concepts.name: Change of Basis
concepts.intuition_gloss: Translate a transformation's description between two coordinate languages — same map, different grid drawn on space.
concepts.formal_gloss: If P is the invertible matrix carrying new-basis coordinates to old, a linear map with matrix A becomes P⁻¹AP in the new basis; matrices related this way are similar.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Change of basis
MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 31: Change of basis; image compression

concepts.name: Characteristic Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ask 'for which stretch factors does the matrix crush some direction flat?' — the answers come packaged as the roots of a single polynomial.
concepts.formal_gloss: The polynomial det(A − λI) of a square matrix A; its roots are exactly the eigenvalues of A, and the multiplicity of each root is that eigenvalue's algebraic multiplicity (versus the geometric multiplicity, the dimension of its eigenspace).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 21: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
Wikipedia — Characteristic polynomial

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 6

concepts.name: Diagonalization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Rotate your viewpoint to the transformation's own special directions, and it becomes nothing but independent stretches.
concepts.formal_gloss: A = PDP⁻¹ with D diagonal, possible iff A has a basis of eigenvectors; D's entries are the eigenvalues.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Eigenvectors and eigenvalues
MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 22: Diagonalization and powers of A

concepts.name: Orthogonal & Unitary Matrices
concepts.intuition_gloss: Transformations that move space rigidly — rotations and reflections that never stretch, shrink, or shear anything.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real matrix Q with QᵀQ = I (complex: U*U = I), i.e. one preserving inner products; such matrices form the groups O(n) and U(n).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 17: Orthogonal matrices and Gram-Schmidt
Wikipedia — Orthogonal matrix

concepts.name: Spectral Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every symmetric transformation is secretly just stretches along some set of perpendicular axes — no rotation or shear hiding anywhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real symmetric (complex self-adjoint) operator has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors with real eigenvalues: A = QΛQᵀ with Q orthogonal, Λ diagonal.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 7
MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 25: Symmetric matrices and positive definiteness

concepts.name: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any transformation whatsoever is a rotation, then pure stretches along perpendicular axes, then another rotation — the universal anatomy of a matrix.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every m×n matrix factors as A = UΣVᵀ with U, V orthogonal and Σ diagonal with nonnegative singular values; the rank-k truncation is the best rank-k approximation in the 2- and Frobenius norms.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 29: Singular value decomposition
Steve Brunton — Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) series
Wikipedia — Singular value decomposition

sources: Brunton & Kutz, Data-Driven Science and Engineering ch. 1

concepts.name: Least Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: When no exact solution exists, take the shadow: find the answer that misses all the equations by the smallest total amount.
concepts.formal_gloss: The minimizer of ||Ax − b||² over x, characterized by the normal equations AᵀAx = Aᵀb, i.e. projecting b orthogonally onto the column space of A.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 16: Projection matrices and least squares
Wikipedia — Least squares

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 4

concepts.name: Quadratic Forms & Positive Definiteness
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bowl-or-saddle shapes you get from squaring and mixing coordinates — positive definite means a true bowl with a single bottom.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function q(x) = xᵀAx for symmetric A; A is positive definite when q(x) > 0 for all x ≠ 0, equivalently all eigenvalues are positive.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 27: Positive definite matrices and minima
Wikipedia — Definite matrix

sources: Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization appendix A

concepts.name: Matrix Factorizations (LU, QR, Cholesky)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Take a matrix apart into simple triangular or rotational pieces, the way you'd factor a number into primes to compute with it faster.
concepts.formal_gloss: Standard decompositions A = LU (elimination), A = QR (orthogonalization), A = LLᵀ (Cholesky, for positive definite A) underpinning practical solution of linear systems.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 4: Factorization into A = LU
Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra
NumPy linalg documentation

concepts.name: Moore–Penrose Pseudoinverse
concepts.intuition_gloss: The best stand-in for an inverse when a true one doesn't exist — undo what the matrix did wherever possible, and land on the closest answer where it isn't.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique matrix A⁺ satisfying the four Penrose conditions; computed from the SVD as A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ, it yields the minimum-norm least-squares solution x = A⁺b of Ax = b.

resources.title: Wikipedia — Moore–Penrose inverse
Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra

concepts.name: Dual Space & Linear Functionals
concepts.intuition_gloss: The space of all measuring devices — every consistent way of reading off a single number from vectors is itself a vector.
concepts.formal_gloss: The space V* of linear maps V → F; finite-dimensional V is isomorphic to V*, and an inner product identifies them canonically (Riesz representation).

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3F
Wikipedia — Dual space

concepts.name: Tensors & Multilinear Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: The machinery for quantities that respond linearly to several inputs at once — matrices generalized to grids of any depth.
concepts.formal_gloss: Elements of tensor product spaces V ⊗ W, equivalently multilinear maps; type-(p,q) tensors transform predictably under change of basis.

resources.title: Wikipedia — Tensor
Eigenchris — Tensors for Beginners (video series)

sources: standard multilinear algebra chapters (Roman, Advanced Linear Algebra)

concepts.name: Cayley–Hamilton Theorem & Minimal Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: Feed a matrix into its own characteristic polynomial and you get zero — every transformation obeys the very equation its stretch factors solve.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every square matrix A satisfies p(A) = 0 where p is its characteristic polynomial; the minimal polynomial of A is the monic polynomial of least degree annihilating A, and it divides the characteristic polynomial.

resources.title: Wikipedia — Cayley–Hamilton theorem
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8

concepts.name: Jordan Normal Form
concepts.intuition_gloss: The honest portrait of a transformation that can't be fully diagonalized — stretches plus unavoidable little shears, arranged in blocks.
concepts.formal_gloss: Over an algebraically closed field, every square matrix is similar to a unique (up to order) block-diagonal matrix of Jordan blocks, classifying operators up to similarity.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8
Wikipedia — Jordan normal form
